(Reference Range-Negative). Imaging Chest xray IMPRESSION: Right middle lobe pneumonia with small bilateral pleural effusions. Patchy opacity in the retrocardiac region likely represents atelectasis CT Chest IMPRESSION: Atelectasis at the right middle lobe, with suggestion of narrowing at the right middle lobe bronchus; however, no definite mass seen on CT. Subtle opacities diffusely in the lung, mostly at the periphery, in the form of due to mild infective changes. Small nodule in the right upper lobe, ground-glass, measuring 3 mm. Soft tissue nodule or adherent mucus in the left main bronchus. Follow up CT or bronchoscopy is recommended. Complete collapse of vertebral T4 spine of uncertain age, correlate with point tenderness. Bilateral pleural effusions, more on the right. MRI and MRA of the head and neck IMPRESSION: Normal MRA of the head. The diminished flow signal seen in both middle cerebra artery branches on projection images appear artifactual as those branches are visualized on the source images. Normal MRA of the neck. There is no acute infarct seen on diffusion images. There is moderate brain atrophy seen. Periventricular hyperintensities indicate moderate changes of small vessel disease. There is a chronic left parietal cortical subcortical infarct identified. There is no midline shift or hydrocephalus. No evidence of acute or chronic blood products. Chest xray Heart size is mildly enlarged, unchanged but its appearance is exaggerated by the presence of low lung volumes. The right middle lobe atelectasis is redemonstrated. Note is also made of significant calcifications of the mitral annulus. There is small amount of bilateral pleural effusion. No pneumothorax is present. Right Upper Extremity Ultrasound IMPRESSION: No evidence of DVT. Antecubital fossa fluid collection; this may represent a joint effusion versus a fluid collection; it is in an atypical location for a bursal cyst. Further imaging is recommended to rule joint effusion. ECHO The left atrial volume is severely increased. Left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and global systolic function are normal (LVEF>55%). Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal. The diameters of aorta at the sinus, ascending and arch levels are normal. The aortic valve leaflets (3) are moderately thickened. There is moderate aortic valve stenosis (valve area 1.0-1.2cm2). No aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. No masses or vegetations are seen on the mitral valve, but cannot be fully excluded due to suboptimal image quality. Mild (1+) mitral regurgitation is seen. [Due to acoustic shadowing, the severity of mitral regurgitation may be significantly underestimated.] There is moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial effusion. IMPRESSION: Moderate calcific aortic stenosis. Normal global and regional biventricular systolic function. Moderate pulmonary hypertension. MRI Right Arm Limited study as described above, demonstrating moderate-sized joint effusion. No bone erosion or articular destruction. This is a nonspecific finding. The possibility of an inflammatory or infectious process cannot be excluded. Nonspecific soft tissue edema. Doubt extra-articular abscess. Findings compatible with tendinosis of the flexor and extensor tendons.

The patient is a postmenopausal woman. She underwent an adrenalectomy in . Following the surgery, she began having spotting until about the end of when she began having heavy vaginal bleeding and was seen in the emergency department. A pelvic ultrasound revealed an enlarged fibroid uterus with a heterogeneous thickened endometrium measuring 14 mm. On she underwent an endometrial biopsy as well as removal of an endocervical polyp and Pap smear. Endometrial biopsy showed an endometrial adenocarcinoma, endometrioid type, FIGO grade 1, arising in the background of EIN. The endocervical polyp was benign. She was counseled regarding her diagnosis and the recommendation for definitive surgical management.

prominence, new from worrisome for lymphadenopathy. Correlation with chest CT advised. CT CHEST Extensive mediastinal and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy is highly concerning for malignancy. Likely diagnoses are lymphoma, bronchogenic carcinoma originating in the right hilus or right lower lobe nodule, or metastatic, extrathoracic malignancy. The large subcarinal node may be the best target for bronchoscopic tissue sampling. Random micronodular and linear opacities, predominantly in the right upper lobe, due to lymphatic involvement with tumor, or lymphostasis due to central adenopathy. CXR Single AP upright portable view of the chest was obtained. Again, there is mediastinal and right hilar enlargement in this patient with mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy seen on CT from . No focal consolidation is seen. There is no large pleural effusion or pneumothorax. Cardiac silhouette is stable. CT CHEST Occlusion of the right upper lobe pulmonary artery and attenuation of the RLL superior segment artery and left main pulmonary artery by mass effect from the extensive mediastinal and hilar adenopathy. No evidence of infarct. Mediastinal and hilar adenopathy along with perisplenic and perihepatic adenopathy appears mildly increased from prior with stranding of surrounding fat. Unchanged 11mm right lower lobe nodule from recent prior. CXR Slight progression of mild pulmonary edema with persistent left basal atelectasis. Asymmetric opacification of the left lung of unclear etiology with density similar to layering effusion. Conventional lateral views may be helpful if the patient can tolerate transport. Path Cell block 19 gauge TBNA #7 C13-15404B TBNA 7, cell block: Positive for malignant cells. Consistent with small cell carcinoma. See note. Note: The tumor cells are immunoreactive for keratin AE1/AE3, CAM 5.2 with some cells showing dot like cytoplasmic staining. They are also immunoreactive for chromogranin , synaptophysin and TTF1. Some of the tumor cells are focally positive for p63. There is no immunoreactivity for CK 7, CK 20, MNF116 or LCA. The focal immunoreactivity for p63 raises the possibility of an additional component of squamous cell carcinoma. Clinical correlation is recommended. Path SPECIMEN: BONE MARROW ASPIRATE AND CORE BIOPSY DIAGNOSIS: HYPERCELLULAR BONE MARROW WITH METASTATIC CARCINOMA. SEE NOTE. Note: The metastatic tumor cells are immunoreactive for chromogranin and TTF-1. Although they are mostly non-immunoreactive for cytokeratin cocktail (AE1/AE3/CAM5.2), and synaptophysin, antigenic loss due to decalcification cannot be excluded. They do not express other lymphoid (CD20 CD3-), stem cell (CD34-) or myeloid (CD117-) markers. See separate pathology report for further characterization. MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION: Peripheral blood smear: The smear is adequate for evaluation. Erythrocytes are normochromic with slight anisopoikilocytosis including rare echinocytes. The white blood count is normal. Large atypical lymphocytes with prominent nucleoli are occasionally seen. The platelet count appears decreased; occasional large platelets are seen. A 200 cell differential shows 44% neutrophils, 1% bands, 36% lymphocytes, 10% monocytes, 3% eosinophils, 0%basophil, 4% metamyelocytes and 2% myelocytes. Aspirate smear: The aspirate material is inadequate for evaluation due to lack of spicules and hemodilution. A limited 200 cell differential shows 0% blasts, 0% promyelocytes, 1% myelocytes, 1% metamyelocytes, 61% bands/neutrophils, 0% eosinophils, 0% erythroids, 34% lymphocytes, 0% plasma cells, 3% monocytes. Clot section and biopsy slides: The core biopsy material is adequate for evaluation. It consists of two fragments measuring overall 1.0 cm core biopsy of trabecular marrow, cortical bone and periosteum with a cellularity of 60-80%. There is an interstitial and intravascular infiltrate of highly atypical cells occurring in clusters occupying 50% of overall cellularity. There is prominent mitosis and apoptosis in this area. In the remaining cellularity, M: E ratio estimate is normal. Erythroid precursors are normal in number and have mildly dyspoietic maturation, including cells with satellite nuclei. Myeloid precursors are normal in number with normal maturation. Megakaryocytes are normal in number with focal loose clustering and include occasional small hypolobated forms. Path Bone Marrow DIAGNOSIS: FLOW CYTOMETRY REPORT FLOW CYTOMETRY IMMUNOPHENOTYPING The following tests (antibodies) were performed: 7, Kappa, Lambda, and CD antigens 2,3,5,7,10,19,20,23 and RESULTS: Three color gating is performed (light scatter vs. CD45) to optimize lymphocyte yield. Lymphocytes comprise ~25% of total analyzed events. Of these, a subset (~5% of total) have lower CD45 expression. Gating on this region demonstrates that these express pan B-cell markers CD19 (dim) along with CD20 (dim). They additionally appear to express CD5 (dim) as well as CD23. They do not express any other characteristic antigens including CD10 or FMC7. The clonality of these B-cells could not be reliably assessed by surface immunoglobulin expression, due to cytophilic staining patterns (i.e. non-specific binding of immunoglobulin light chain antibodies), a pattern which persists despite repeat washings. In the CD45-bright region, there is a smaller population of admixed B-cells with the normal bright CD20 expression. T cells comprise 66% of all lymphoid events and express mature lineage antigens. INTERPRETATION Immunophenotypic findings show the presence of an abnormal B-cell population with co-expression of CD5 and CD23 suspicious for involvement by a B cell lymphoproliferative disorder. The presence of cytophilic antibody precludes determination of clonality. However the immunophenotype (CLL-like) is suggestive of monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis. Please correlate with clinical, morphologic and other ancillary findings. Note: This test was performed using analyte specific reagents (ASRs). These ASRs have not been cleared or approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). However, the FDA has determined that such clearance or approval is not necessary This test was developed and its performance characteristics determined by the Flow Cytometry Laboratory at which is licensed by to perform high complexity tests. This test was used for clinical purposes; it should not be regarded as for research. Path SPECIMEN SUBMITTED: immunophenotyping - EBUS/TBNA, 7 DIAGNOSIS: FLOW CYTOMETRY REPORT FLOW CYTOMETRY IMMUNOPHENOTYPING The following tests (antibodies) were performed: 7, Kappa, Lambda, and CD antigens 2,3,7,8,10,19,20 and RESULTS: Three color gating is performed (light scatter vs. CD45) to optimize lymphocyte yield. CD45-bright, low side scatter gated lymphocytes are extremely scant. A majority of the analyzed events are in the CD45-dim, variable side-scatter region. INTERPRETATION Non-diagnostic study. Cell marker analysis was attempted, but was non-diagnostic in this case due to insufficient number of lymphoid cells for analysis. Review of corresponding cytospin preparations from material submitted to flow cytometry shows marked bacterial overgrowth and background clusters of atypical cells in a hemorrhagic background. Correlation with clinical findings and morphology (see separate pathology report) is recommended. Flow cytometry immunophenotyping may not detect all abnormal populations due to topography, sampling or artifacts of sample preparation. IMAGING: ======== MRI HEAD IMPRESSION: Gyriform pattern of slow diffusion in the frontoparietal lobes near the vertex could reflect cerebritis, particularly given the underlying subcortical signal abnormality seen on T2 and FLAIR images, and T2/FLAIR signal abnormalities in the occipital lobes. The differential diagnosis also includes a posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). Multiple foci of susceptibility signal in both cerebellar hemispheres, along the occipital lobes, and along the frontal lobes near the vertex probably reflect hemosiderin, and could potentially reflect hemorrhagic metastases although the postcontrast images are severely limited due to patient motion. Repeating contrast-enhanced MRI images of the brain when the patient is able to be still would be helpful in further characterization of these lesions. Numerous lesions throughout the calvarium, clivus, and imaged upper cervical spine likely reflect osseous metastases.
